patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch is a response to an issue where the btrfs file system is experiencing errors when trying to free extents, resulting in a '-2 No such entry' error. The developer, Boris Burkov, is seeking more information about how the quota was being managed before the error occurred, specifically whether subvolumes or qgroups were manually deleted.

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the system was experiencing crashes due to btrfs quota issues, and provided details about their specific setup, including nightly snapshots, subvolume deletion, and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots. They mentioned that they have quotas still running on a single host but turned off balance and scrub jobs as a workaround.
review_comments.raw_body:
All 3 filesystems this happened on were btrfs receive recipients, so
that created snapshots nightly (when it crashed each time).
Also after btrfs receive, my script deletes the oldest backup that rolls
out the rotation.
Then I also have some amount btrfs-snaps that makes hourly/daily/weekly
snapshots

So yes, multiple per day per subvolume multipled by many subvolume
(10-ish)

I have squotas still running on a single host but I turned off btrfs
balance nightly cronjob as well as btrfs scrub, and it has not crashed
yet (it's a 20TB+ backup recipient built from scratch, I woudl love for
squotas to work on it)

Marc
--

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Marc MERLIN encountered an error when trying to remove the quota on a Btrfs filesystem, receiving an errno=-2 No such entry error in do_free_extent_accounting. He was able to resolve the issue by canceling the balance operation and then re-converting the filesystem to block group trees.
review_comments.raw_body:
Took a while to remove the quota, but once it did:

moremagic:/tmp# mount -o rw,enospc_debug,skip_balance /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
moremagic:/tmp# cd /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
moremagic:/mnt/btrfs_bigbackup# btrfs balance cancel `pwd`
BTRFS info (device dm-0): setting compat-ro feature flag for FREE_SPACE_TREE (0x1)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): setting compat-ro feature flag for FREE_SPACE_TREE_VALID (0x2)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): balance: resume skipped
BTRFS info (device dm-0): checking UUID tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling ssd optimizations
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling free space tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): balance: canceled

And now I'm good. I can re-re-convert to block group trees (took hours
overnight to go back):
moremagic:~# btrfstune --convert-to-block-group-tree  /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0
Converted the filesystem to block group tree feature

moremagic:~# mount /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 /mnt/btrfs_bigbackup
BTRFS: device label DS6 devid 1 transid 298273 /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 (251:0) scanned by mount (1030419)
BTRFS info (device dm-0): first mount of filesystem a97dec85-a0d5-42ab-a0ef-e9b7479fbe43
BTRFS info (device dm-0): using crc32c (crc32c-generic) checksum algorithm
BTRFS info (device dm-0): forcing free space tree for sector size 4096 with page size 16384
BTRFS warning (device dm-0): read-write for sector size 4096 with page size 16384 is experimental
BTRFS info (device dm-0): bdev /dev/mapper/crypt_bcache0 errs: wr 0, rd 0, flush 0, corrupt 5074, gen 0
BTRFS info (device dm-0): checking UUID tree
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling ssd optimizations
BTRFS info (device dm-0): enabling free space tree

I'm back in business, thank you.

Will definitely keep squota off for this one. Thanks again about the
--remove-simple-quota suggestion, of all the things I tried, this is one
I hadn't.

Marc

Successfully cleared ino cache
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount -o rw,clear_cache,enospc_debug,skip_balance LABEL=btrfs_pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs quota disable /mnt/btrfs_pool4

sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount /dev/mapper/pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# cd /mnt/btrfs_pool4

Done, had to relocate 0 out of 2008 chunks
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# 

btrfs tune would have likely also worked to remove squota, but the trick
after that is mounting with skip_balance or otherwise, even after quota
removal, balance restarts right away and puts the FS read only again
if squota isn't disabled first.

on my laptop. It looks like squota and balance are simply not compatible until the
fix for squota, lands.
Now that I know more, I rescued the FS and removed squota on my own and it's good again.

It looks pretty clear that btrfs balance seems to trigger the bug in

For google and people who find that thread, the quicker recovery is:
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs rescue clear-ino-cache  /dev/mapper/pool4
(...)
Successfully cleaned up ino cache for root id: 108518
Successfully cleared ino cache
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount -o rw,clear_cache,enospc_debug,skip_balance LABEL=btrfs_pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# btrfs quota disable /mnt/btrfs_pool4

sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# mount /dev/mapper/pool4 /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool3# cd /mnt/btrfs_pool4
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# btrfs balance start -dusage=20  `pwd`
Done, had to relocate 0 out of 2008 chunks
sauron:/mnt/btrfs_pool4# 

btrfs tune would have likely also worked to remove squota, but the trick
after that is mounting with skip_balance or otherwise, even after quota
removal, balance restarts right away and puts the FS read only again
if squota isn't disabled first.

Marc

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the system was experiencing crashes due to btrfs quota issues, and provided details about their specific setup, including nightly snapshots, subvolume deletion, and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots. They mentioned that they have quotas still running on a single host but turned off balance and scrub jobs as a workaround.
review_comments.raw_body:
All 3 filesystems this happened on were btrfs receive recipients, so
that created snapshots nightly (when it crashed each time).
Also after btrfs receive, my script deletes the oldest backup that rolls
out the rotation.
Then I also have some amount btrfs-snaps that makes hourly/daily/weekly
snapshots

So yes, multiple per day per subvolume multipled by many subvolume
(10-ish)

I have squotas still running on a single host but I turned off btrfs
balance nightly cronjob as well as btrfs scrub, and it has not crashed
yet (it's a 20TB+ backup recipient built from scratch, I woudl love for
squotas to work on it)

Marc

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 7/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qrwlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to queued rwlocks, allowing for tracing of lock releases that occur while the lock is being contended by other threads. The implementation uses an out-of-line traced unlock approach similar to that used for queued spinlocks. This allows for more detailed debugging and analysis of concurrent access patterns in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 6/7] locking: Factor out __queued_read_unlock()/__queued_write_unlock()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the __queued_read_unlock() and __queued_write_unlock() functions out of the queued rwlock implementation, preparing for the addition of contended release tracepoint instrumentation.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a contended_release tracepoint to the qspinlock implementation, allowing for tracing of lock contention events. The tracepoint is implemented using the arch-overridable queued_spin_release() function, which ensures correct behavior across all architectures. The patch also includes changes to the hot path to minimize performance impact and provides binary size estimates.

review_comments.author: Paul E. McKenney
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch, acknowledging that it's a nice split out of functionality.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 4/7] locking: Factor out queued_spin_release()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called `queued_spin_release()` which is an arch-overridable unlock primitive. It renames existing arch-specific overrides of `queued_spin_unlock()` to use the new name, making it easier for future changes to be made without affecting existing code. The change does not introduce any functional differences and is intended as a preparatory step for further modifications.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 2/7] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original function is simplified and no longer contains the slowpath, which is moved to the new function. This change avoids increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint in an inline function. The new function is exported as a symbol for use elsewhere in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 3/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking subsystem. The contended_release tracepoint fires on the holder side when a contended lock is released, allowing for correlation of lock hold time under contention with waiter events by lock address. The patch adds calls to the trace_contended_release() function in the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, rwsems, and percpu-rwsems. This enables more detailed tracing and debugging of locking behavior in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 1/7] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement from the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events in this file reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/7] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new tracepoint called contended_release that fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides additional information such as hold time estimation and the holder's stack at release time, which can be used to measure how long the lock was held under contention. The patch also updates the behavior of reader/writer locks to fire the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting, not just the last reader. The series includes several preparatory commits to split and refactor existing code.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes the requirement for FUSE_DAX_FMAP to be advertised in perpetuity, even if CONFIG_FUSE_DAX is disabled. It uses capability bits to control support for DAX, similar to cpuid capability bits. The goal is to prevent over-genericization of the interface and allow for more targeted solutions.

review_comments.author: Dan Williams
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dan Williams suggested that instead of making the interface more generic, the kernel should clearly indicate which methods are available and allow for a deprecation period before removing native support. He proposed adding a capability bit or similar mechanism to make it clear that native support might be removed in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right, if the protocol on day one is "user space must ask which method is available", then userspace can not be surprised when one option disappears. So to give time for the bpf approach to mature the kernel can do something like "famfs and bpf  mapping support are available". In some future kernel the famfs native option disappears after a deprecation period. 

When folks ask 10 years from now why this ever supported optionality the explanation is "oh because famfs enjoyed first mover advantage to prove out fs semantics layered on dax devices", or "turns out there are some cases where bpf is not fast enough but it still stops the proliferation of more in kernel mapping implementations".

Something like FUSE_DAX_FMAP is always available but the backend to that is optionally native vs bpf. ...or some other arrangement to make it clear that native might be gone someday.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Darrick Wong expressed concern that the patch could break fuse servers if it removes GET_FMAP in favor of IOMAP_BEGIN without also updating libfuse to translate ->get_fmap implementations into ->iomap_begin, which would require dealing with vendored libfuse versions.

Reviewer Darrick Wong noted that famfs requires the ability to express formulaic meta-mapping, which cannot be efficiently achieved using traditional iomap parlance and individual iomaps; he believes there's no good reason to remove GET_FMAP at this time.

Reviewer Darrick Wong suggested a different approach to resolving the interface generality issue by merging fuse-iomap after famfs and extending the existing fuse-iomap-bpf prototype to allow per-mount and per-inode iomap bpf ops. This would enable thorough analysis of performance characteristics for various approaches, including using GET_FMAP as-is, uploading raw iomaps, or uploading custom bpf programs.

reviewer emphasized the importance of testing and gathering user feedback before making changes to the interface, suggesting that shipping the new mechanism to users is necessary for determining the best approach
review_comments.raw_body:
So don't break fuse servers.  If you wanted to (say) get rid of
GET_FMAP in favor of IOMAP_BEGIN, you could alter libfuse to translate a
fuse server's ->get_fmap implementation into the equivalent
->iomap_begin, and eventually the kernel can stop making GET_FMAP calls
to userspace.

The trouble here is that I've also seen half a dozen projects vendoring
libfuse so that's a nightmare that will have to be dealt with.  But
maybe that doesn't even matter, because...

---

...the memory interleaving is a rather interesting quality of famfs.
There's no good way to express a formulaic meta-mapping in traditional
iomap parlance, and famfs needs that to interleave across memory
controllers/dimm boxen/whatever.  Throwing individual iomaps at the
kernel is a very inefficient way to do that.  So I don't think there's a
good reason to get rid of GET_FMAP at this time...

---

...however the strongest case (IMO) would be if (having merged famfs) we
then merge fuse-iomap after famfs.  Then we extend the existing
fuse-iomap-bpf prototype to allow per-mount and per-inode iomap bpf ops.
That enables us to analyze thoroughly the performance characteristics of:

a) Using GET_FMAP as-is

b) Uploading raw iomaps (HA)

c) Uploading a single bpf program to make iomaps, exchanging fmap-style
mapping data into a bpf map, and having the single bpf program walk
through the map

d) Uploading a custom bpf program per famfs file to make iomaps.  No
bpfmap required, but the setup and compilation are now much more complex

Then we'll finally know which approach is the best, having broken the
Gordian Knot of how to merge famfs and fuse-iomap.

If we decide that (c) or (d) are actually better, then guess what?  To
get any of the iomap functionality, you have to set an inode flag, and
that (FUSE_CAP_FAMFS && FUSE_CAP_IOMAP && FUSE_ATTR_IOMAP) is the signal
for "don't call GET_FMAP".  FUSE_CAP_FAMFS && (!FUSE_CAP_IOMAP ||
!FUSE_ATTR_IOMAP) means "call GET_FMAP".

Yes, we burn a couple of fuse command values to find out, but that's all.

(TBH I still dislike GET_DAXDEV, that really should just be another
application of backing files, and the backing file id gets passed to
GET_FMAP.)

What do you all think of doing that?

---

Yes.  We're not *capable* of determining the best mechanism unless we
can start shipping these things to users to get their feedback.  Only
then can we iterate and make real improvements.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Joanne Koong expressed concern that relying on capability bits for deprecation may not be sufficient, as it does not address the issue of breaking users who are on older versions of libfuse if they upgrade to a newer kernel. She also questioned the deprecation period and process, asking what timeframe is required before a feature can be fully removed.

Reviewer Joanne Koong suggested making the interleaving part of the interface generic by adding an interleave descriptor to the user API, citing examples from other storage technologies such as RAID-0 and md-stripe.

Reviewer Joanne Koong expressed concerns that the GET_FMAP interface is too specific and may not be suitable for other interleaved or striped layouts, requesting it to be made more generic.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think it's this simple. We can't assume libfuse is the only
way servers talk to the kernel. Some servers use the /dev/fuse
interface directly. And, as I understand it, this would still break
users who are on older versions of libfuse if they upgrade to a newer
kernel.

My reason for pushing back isn't because I don't want this to work; I
just want to make sure that if we're going to rely on this as a safety
hatch, then we can actually do it.

Going back to what Dan said about using the capability bits for
deprecation, "In some future kernel the famfs native option disappears
after a deprecation period" - what does the deprecation period/process
look like? Do you have to wait a certain amount of time before it can
be fully removed or is it pretty immediate?

---

So could we make the interleaving part generic then? Striped /
interleaved layouts are used elsewhere (eg RAID-0, md-stripe, etc.) -
could we add a generic interleave descriptor to the uapi and use that
for what famfs needs?

---

To be completely honest, this is orthogonal to what I was hoping we
could discuss on this thread. My main concern is the GET_FMAP part.
Can we make it more generic to other interleaved/striped layouts?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Joanne Koong questioned whether the interface is already generic and reusable for other dax-backed servers, despite being named famfs-specific, citing concerns that the naming conventions are implementation-specific.

review_comments.author: Darrick Wong
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the feasibility of making the interface more generic, citing concerns about performance overhead and the difficulty of finding a one-size-fits-all solution.

Reviewer Darrick Wong questioned whether a name change and capability bit could solve the issue of over-genericizing the interface, pointing out that md-raid presents a unified LBA address space, allowing filesystems to be unaware of translations underneath it.

Reviewer questioned the need for an overly generic interface, citing lack of evidence that it's actually needed and expressing concern that it would invite over-generalization.
review_comments.raw_body:
That depends on how much gluecode you can stand up to redirect older
programs.

---

I doubt it.  md-raid presents a unified LBA address space, which means
that the filesystem doesn't have to know anything about whatever
translations might happen underneath it.  Even memory controllers
quietly take care of striping across DIMMs and whatnot.

Most filesystems that implement striping themselves don't restrict
themselves to monotonically increasing LBA ranges rotored across each
device like md-raid0 does.

But for whatever reason, pmem/dax don't have remapping layers like
md/dm so filesystems have to do that on their own if the hardware
doesn't do it for them.

---

"Generic"... do we even /have/ a second user?  I don't feel like we do.

--D

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 00/25] nfs4.1: add some directory delegation testcases
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds new testcases for directory delegation in the NFSv4.1 protocol, including tests for CB_NOTIFY behavior and various operations that trigger dir delegation recall.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patch series adds a comprehensive set of testcases for directory delegation in NFSv4.1, including tests for CB_NOTIFY behavior and various operations that trigger dir delegation recall.
review_comments.raw_body:
This flag has been proposed as part of RFC8881bis. This flag is used to
negotiate extensions to the original directory delegations originally
specified in RFC8881.

In practice, the Linux nfs server requires that the client support this
flag if it's requesting anything other than a recall-only delegation.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x | 3 ++-
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x b/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x

+++ b/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
@@ -3611,7 +3611,8 @@ enum notify_type4 {
         NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY = 2,
         NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY = 3,
         NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY = 4,
-        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5
+        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5,
+        NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND = 6 /* proposed in rfc8881bis */
 };
 
 /* Changed entry information.  */

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 01/25] nfs4.1: add proposed NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, to the NFSv4 protocol as proposed in RFC8881bis. The flag is used to negotiate extensions to directory delegations and is required by the Linux NFS server for non-recall-only delegations. This change updates the xdrdef/nfs4.x file to include the new enum value.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 02/25] nfs4.1: add a getfh() to the end of create_obj() compound
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a GETFH operation to the end of the create_obj() compound in the NFSv4.1 server, allowing for the retrieval of the filehandle of newly created objects. This is necessary because some tests require knowledge of the filehandle of the created object. The change affects the environment.py file in the server41tests directory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 03/25] server41tests: add a basic GET_DIR_DELEGATION test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a basic test for directory delegation in the NFSv4 server, specifically testing the handout, recall, and return of directory delegations. The test is implemented using the existing server41tests framework and involves creating a session, opening a file, and then performing operations to test the delegation functionality. The patch also includes additional code to decode notify events and convert bitmaps to integers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 04/25] server41tests: add a test for duplicate GET_DIR_DELEGATION requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the NFSv4.1 implementation, specifically testing how the server handles duplicate GET_DIR_DELEGATION requests. The test checks that the server returns GDD4_UNAVAIL when it receives a duplicate request, which is the expected behavior according to the NFSv4.1 protocol specification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 05/25] server41tests: pass_warn() when server doesn't support dir delegations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the server41tests to pass a warning instead of failing when the server doesn't support directory delegations or doesn't hand out a delegation. It also adds checks for correct notification handling and passes a warning if the server doesn't offer the necessary notifications. This change makes it safe to keep the directory delegation tests in the 'all' group.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 06/25] server41tests: test remove triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test case to the server41tests suite, which verifies that removing a file from one client triggers a delegation recall on another client. The test creates a directory delegation without notification, opens a file in it, and then removes the file from a second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 07/25] server41tests: test rename triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when a file is renamed from a second client, it triggers a directory delegation recall on the first client. The test creates a directory delegation with no notification mask, then creates a file and renames it from the second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 08/25] server41tests: test mkdir triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that creating a subdirectory from a second client triggers a directory delegation recall on the server. The test first gets a directory delegation without notification, then creates a new subdirectory from another client, and finally checks if the server issues a CB_RECALL. This ensures that the server properly recalls the delegation when a new subdirectory is created under a delegated directory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 09/25] server41tests: test link triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test case to the server41tests suite, which verifies that a directory delegation recall is triggered when a file is hard-linked from another client. The test creates a dir delegation with no notification mask, then creates a file and links it to a new name from a second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response. This patch aims to ensure that the NFSv4.1 server correctly handles directory delegation recalls triggered by link operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 10/25] server41tests: test no notifications without GFLAG_EXTEND
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when a file is created in a directory for which a delegation has been requested, the server issues a CB_RECALL instead of a CB_NOTIFY if the NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag is not set. The test requests a delegation with the NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY flag but without the NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag and checks that the expected callback is received.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 11/25] server41tests: test unrequested notification type triggers recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when an unrequested notification type is triggered, the server issues a CB_RECALL instead of a CB_NOTIFY. The test creates a directory delegation and requests only REMOVE notifications, then triggers an ADD event from a second client. It checks that the server responds with a CB_RECALL in this scenario.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 12/25] server41tests: add a test for removal from dir with dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, specifically testing the removal of a file from a directory while delegation is in place. The test requests a dir delegation with REMOVE_ENTRY notifications and verifies that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct REMOVE event. Additionally, the patch updates the GET_DIR_DELEGATION request to include full child and directory attribute bitmaps for all CB_NOTIFY tests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 13/25] server41tests: add a test for directory add notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite for directory delegation, specifically testing that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with an ADD event when a file is created from another client while a dir delegation is active.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 14/25] server41tests: add test for RENAME event notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite for verifying that the NFSv4.1 server sends correct RENAME event notifications when a file is renamed from one client to another. The test creates a directory delegation that accepts notification of RENAME events, and then renames a file from one client to another. It verifies that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct RENAME event details.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 15/25] server41tests: verify child attributes in ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to verify that child attributes are included in the ADD notification sent by the server when a new file is created. The test creates a directory delegation, opens a file from a second client, and checks that the ADD notification includes the correct size attribute for the new file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 16/25] server41tests: test CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests module in the Linux kernel's NFSv4 implementation. The test, called 'testDirDelegDirAttrs', verifies that the server sends a CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS notification when directory attributes are changed. To do this, it requests a delegation with specific notifications enabled, creates a file from another client, and checks for the presence of a CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS event among the changes received by the first client. The test also checks that the notification contains valid directory attributes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 17/25] server41tests: test mkdir triggers ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that when a client creates a subdirectory on a delegated directory, the server sends an ADD notification to the delegating client.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 18/25] server41tests: test DELEGRETURN stops notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that notifications are stopped when a delegation is returned using the DELEGRETURN operation. The test gets a directory delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications, immediately returns it, and then creates a file from a second client. It checks that no callbacks are received after the delegation was returned.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 19/25] server41tests: verify filehandle in ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that the filehandle attribute is included in ADD notifications and matches the filehandle obtained via GETFH. The test creates a file from a second client, records its filehandle, and then requests a dir delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications. It checks that the ADD notification includes a filehandle attribute that matches the one obtained via GETFH.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 20/25] server41tests: test cross-directory rename REMOVE notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when renaming a file across directories, the source directory's delegation receives a NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY notification instead of a NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY notification. The test creates a delegated directory, a target directory, and then renames a file from the delegated directory to the target directory using a separate client session.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 21/25] server41tests: test cross-directory rename ADD notification on target
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite in the NFSv4.1 implementation. The test, called 'testDirDelegCrossRenameTarget', verifies that when a file is renamed across directories, an ADD notification is sent to the target directory's delegation. This behavior is specified in RFC 8881bis Section 27.4.6. The test creates a source directory and file, obtains a delegation on the target directory, renames the file into the delegated directory, and then checks that the correct notifications were sent.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 22/25] server41tests: test link triggers ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that creating a hard link triggers an ADD notification on the directory's delegation. The test creates a file in a delegated directory, then links it to a new name from another client session. It checks that the server sends an ADD notification with the correct entry name when the link is created.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 23/25] server41tests: test same-client changes don't trigger notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the NFSv4.1 implementation. The test, called 'testDirDelegSameClientNoNotify', verifies that changes made by a delegation-holding session itself do not trigger CB_NOTIFY or CB_RECALL callbacks. This is in accordance with RFC 8881bis Section 16.2.11.2, which states that order-unaware clients should not receive notifications for changes they made themselves.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v2 24/25] server41tests: test cross-directory rename-over nad_old_entry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when a cross-directory rename overwrites an existing file in a delegated directory, the server reports the overwritten entry via nad_old_entry in the NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY notification. The patch also fixes _getDirDeleg to initialize cb.changes as a list and use extend() to accumulate notifications, allowing tests to receive multiple CB_NOTIFY callbacks and see all notifications.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the Linux kernel's NFSv4 implementation. The test, called 'testDirDelegRenameOver', verifies that when a within-directory rename operation overwrites an existing file entry, the server correctly populates the nad_old_entry field in the NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY notification with the overwritten entry's information. This is in accordance with RFC 8881bis Section 27.4.6. The test creates two files in a delegated directory, renames one of them over the other, and then checks that the server sends a correct RENAME notification with the old entry's information.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 28/28] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 28/28] nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir attribute changes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 00/28] vfs/nfsd: add support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for CB_NOTIFY callbacks in directory delegations, allowing clients to register interest in a directory and receive notifications about changes within it without losing its lease. The patches include bugfixes, new tracepoints, and the addition of a new FSNOTIFY_EVENT_RENAME data type to handle cross-directory rename events. The series builds on previous work on directory delegation and has been tested with pynfs, but further client-side implementation is needed for complete testing.

review_comments.raw_body:

+#define show_lease_break_flags(val)					\

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about how to determine which directory change events should be ignored by nfsd, and responded by adding a new routine (inode_lease_ignore_mask) that returns the union of all ignored inode events for an inode. The routine walks the list of leases, or'ing together their FL_IGN_DIR_* bits. This addresses the issue of how to configure the fsnotify_mark mask based on existing leases.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the server recalling directory delegations when a client requests notifications for directory change events. The author agreed to add support for ignoring lease breaks on directory changes by introducing new flags and modifying existing functions.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a request for additional debugging information to understand how the CB_NOTIFY callbacks are being called. They have added a tracepoint to fsnotify.c and modified the include/trace/events/fsnotify.h file to display the event details in the trace output.
review_comments.raw_body:
Add a tracepoint so we can see exactly how this is being called.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 fs/notify/fsnotify.c            |  5 ++++
 include/trace/events/fsnotify.h | 51 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

diff --git a/fs/notify/fsnotify.c b/fs/notify/fsnotify.c

--- a/fs/notify/fsnotify.c
+++ b/fs/notify/fsnotify.c
@@ -14,6 +14,9 @@
 #include <linux/fsnotify_backend.h>
 #include "fsnotify.h"
 
+#define CREATE_TRACE_POINTS
+#include <trace/events/fsnotify.h>
+
 /*
  * Clear all of the marks on an inode when it is being evicted from core
  */
@@ -504,6 +507,8 @@ int fsnotify(__u32 mask, const void *data, int data_type, struct inode *dir,
 	int ret = 0;
 	__u32 test_mask, marks_mask = 0;
 
+	trace_fsnotify(mask, data, data_type, dir, file_name, inode, cookie);
+
 	if (path)
 		mnt = real_mount(path->mnt);
 
diff --git a/include/trace/events/fsnotify.h b/include/trace/events/fsnotify.h
new file mode 100644
index 000000000000..341bbd57a39b
--- /dev/null
+++ b/include/trace/events/fsnotify.h
@@ -0,0 +1,51 @@
+/* SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0 */
+#undef TRACE_SYSTEM
+#define TRACE_SYSTEM fsnotify
+
+#if !defined(_TRACE_FSNOTIFY_H) || defined(TRACE_HEADER_MULTI_READ)
+#define _TRACE_FSNOTIFY_H
+
+#include <linux/tracepoint.h>
+
+#include <trace/misc/fsnotify.h>
+
+TRACE_EVENT(fsnotify,
+	TP_PROTO(__u32 mask, const void *data, int data_type,
+		 struct inode *dir, const struct qstr *file_name,
+		 struct inode *inode, u32 cookie),
+
+	TP_ARGS(mask, data, data_type, dir, file_name, inode, cookie),
+
+	TP_STRUCT__entry(
+		__field(__u32, mask)
+		__field(unsigned long, dir_ino)

+		__field(dev_t, s_dev)
+		__field(int, data_type)
+		__field(u32, cookie)
+		__string(file_name, file_name ? (const char *)file_name->name : "")
+	),

+	TP_fast_assign(

+		__entry->dir_ino = dir ? dir->i_ino : 0;
+		__entry->ino = inode ? inode->i_ino : 0;
+		__entry->s_dev = dir ? dir->i_sb->s_dev :
+				 inode ? inode->i_sb->s_dev : 0;
+		__entry->data_type = data_type;
+		__entry->cookie = cookie;
+		__assign_str(file_name);
+	),
+
+	TP_printk("dev=%d:%d dir=%lu ino=%lu data_type=%d cookie=0x%x mask=0x%x %s name=%s",
+		  MAJOR(__entry->s_dev), MINOR(__entry->s_dev),
+		  __entry->dir_ino, __entry->ino,
+		  __entry->data_type, __entry->cookie,
+		  __entry->mask, show_fsnotify_mask(__entry->mask),
+		  __get_str(file_name))
+);
+
+#endif /* _TRACE_FSNOTIFY_H */
+
+/* This part must be outside protection */
+#include <trace/define_trace.h>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about modifying the mask on an existing mark when new directory delegations are set or unset, and responded by adding a new exported function that allows callers to set and clear bits in the mark's mask and does recalculation if something changed.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the lack of information provided to fsnotify consumers when a rename operation replaces an existing file, by introducing a new FSNOTIFY_EVENT_RENAME data type that carries both the moved dentry and the overwritten target inode. The author updated several functions to handle this new type, including fsnotify_move(), fsnotify_data_inode(), fsnotify_data_dentry(), and fsnotify_data_sb(). This change allows fsnotify consumers like nfsd to atomically observe the overwritten file without needing a separate FS_DELETE event.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the nfs_breaker_owns_lease() function, which was incorrectly checking if the lease breaker owns the lease. The author agreed to add a check for nfsd leases by verifying that fl_lmops is set to nfsd_lease_mng_ops.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the removal of NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE and NFS4_FHSIZE from the UAPI headers, explaining that these constants are defined by the RFC and should be obtained from more authoritative sources.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that it's unusual for the NFS protocol to report both the original and overwritten nodes in a single rename event, but accepted the design choice.
review_comments.raw_body:
It is strange to me that the NFS protocol needs to report the overwritten
node in the same event of the rename, but oh well, fine by me.

Feel free to add:
Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged the usefulness of including unnecessary information in the protocol, citing the historical context and limitations of RFC5661.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah, it's not very useful, but the protocol requires it. Unfortunately
RFC5661 was written before anyone had made a real implementation of
directory delegations. If we were rewriting it today, we'd probably
make that info optional.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 01/28] filelock: pass current blocking lease to trace_break_lease_block() rather than "new_fl"
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the filelock code to pass the current blocking lease to the trace_break_lease_block() function instead of a new lease, allowing for more accurate tracing information. The change also ensures that the trace_break_lease_unblock() function is passed a NULL pointer when the original lease may have been freed, preventing potential deception in the tracing output.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 02/28] filelock: add support for ignoring deleg breaks for dir change events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support to the Linux filelock system for ignoring delegation breaks on directory change events in NFS clients. It introduces a new flags parameter to try_break_deleg() and modifies __break_lease to ignore certain types of delegation break events, allowing the client to be notified of changes after the fact instead of recalling the delegation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 03/28] filelock: add a tracepoint to start of break_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a tracepoint to the start of the break_lease() function in the Linux kernel's filelock module. The tracepoint is used to show the LEASE_BREAK_* flags that are passed to the function. This allows for easier debugging and analysis of lease breaking operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 04/28] filelock: add an inode_lease_ignore_mask helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `inode_lease_ignore_mask` to the Linux kernel's filelock subsystem. The function takes an inode as input and returns a mask of all directory change events that are currently ignored by any leases associated with that inode. This is useful for NFSd, which can use this information to configure the fsnotify_mark mask. The patch introduces a new routine that walks through the list of leases on the given inode, or'ing together their ignored event flags. If all possible events are already ignored, the function returns immediately without iterating over the rest of the lease list.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 05/28] fsnotify: new tracepoint in fsnotify()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint to the fsnotify function, allowing developers to see exactly how it is being called. The tracepoint includes various fields such as the file system mask, data type, inode information, and cookie value. This will aid in debugging and understanding the behavior of the fsnotify function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 06/28] fsnotify: add fsnotify_modify_mark_mask()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called fsnotify_modify_mark_mask() to the Linux kernel's fsnotify subsystem. The function allows callers to modify an existing mark's mask by setting and clearing specific bits, and also updates the associated connector if necessary. This is required for NFSd to manage directory delegations correctly. The function takes a mark pointer, a set of bits to be added to the mask, and a set of bits to be cleared from the mask as parameters.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 07/28] fsnotify: add FSNOTIFY_EVENT_RENAME data type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new data type FSNOTIFY_EVENT_RENAME to the fsnotify system, which allows consumers like nfsd to atomically observe the overwritten file when a rename operation replaces an existing entry. The new data type carries both the moved dentry and the inode that was overwritten by the rename. The patch updates various functions in the fsnotify system to handle this new data type, including fsnotify_move(), fsnotify_data_inode(), and fsnotify_data_dentry(). This change enables more efficient handling of rename operations and improves the accuracy of notifications for file system events.

review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah, it's not very useful, but the protocol requires it. Unfortunately
RFC5661 was written before anyone had made a real implementation of
directory delegations. If we were rewriting it today, we'd probably
make that info optional.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 08/28] nfsd: check fl_lmops in nfsd_breaker_owns_lease()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a quick check to the nfsd_breaker_owns_lease() function to verify that the lease being checked is an nfsd lease by checking if its fl_lmops field matches the nfsd_lease_mng_ops structure. This simplifies the function and improves performance by avoiding unnecessary checks for leases not created by nfsd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 09/28] nfsd: add protocol support for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the CB_NOTIFY protocol in the nfsd kernel module. It updates the xdr (external data representation) files to include the necessary definitions and removes duplicate definitions from other headers. The patch also regenerates the xdr files, which caused conflicts with existing definitions of NFS4_VERIFIER_SIZE and NFS4_FHSIZE. These constants are now removed from the kernel API and userspace consumers should obtain them from more authoritative sources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 10/28] nfs_common: add new NOTIFY4_* flags proposed in RFC8881bis
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new NOTIFY4_* flags to the NFSv4 protocol as proposed in RFC8881bis. The new flags include NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, NOTIFY4_AUFLAG_VALID, and others that extend the functionality of the GET_DIR_DELEGATION operation. These changes are necessary to support the updated protocol specification. The patch modifies several files, including the XDR specification file and the NFSv4xdr_gen.c file, to include these new flags.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 11/28] nfsd: allow nfsd to get a dir lease with an ignore mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the NFS daemon to obtain a directory lease with an ignore mask, enabling it to filter out specific notification types during the lease request process.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 12/28] nfsd: update the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the fsnotify mark when setting or removing a directory delegation in the NFS server. It introduces a new helper function, nfsd_fsnotify_recalc_mask, which recalculates the mask on the nfsd_file's fsnotify_mark based on the current directory delegations on an inode. The function is called when directory delegations are added or removed, ensuring that notifications for relevant events are properly set up or cleared. This patch addresses a specific scenario where directory delegation events were not being properly notified to clients, leading to potential issues with file system consistency and client-side caching.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 13/28] nfsd: make nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation bool return
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the nfsd4_callback_ops->prepare operation to return a boolean value indicating whether processing should continue or not. The change is made to handle allocation failures during CB_NOTIFY operations by stopping callback processing if an allocation fails.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 14/28] nfsd: add callback encoding and decoding linkages for CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds routines for encoding and decoding CB_NOTIFY messages in the NFSv4 callback protocol. It introduces new functions to handle the encoding and decoding of these messages, which are generated by the xdrgen tool. The patch also updates the nfsd/nfs4callback.c file to include the new encoding and decoding functions, as well as adds a new procedure for CB_NOTIFY to the rpc_procinfo array. This patch is part of a larger series of changes to improve the NFSv4 callback protocol.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 15/28] nfsd: use RCU to protect fi_deleg_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch uses RCU (Read-Copy-Update) to protect the fi_deleg_file pointer in the nfsd kernel module. The issue it solves is a race condition where concurrent accesses from workqueue callbacks can dereference a NULL pointer if they occur during delegation teardown. To fix this, the patch annotates fi_deleg_file with __rcu and converts all accessors to use RCU primitives such as rcu_assign_pointer() and rcu_dereference_protected(). This prepares for a subsequent patch that will safely acquire a reference from the CB_NOTIFY callback path without holding the fi_lock. The approach taken is to use RCU to ensure that the fi_deleg_file pointer remains valid even when it is being updated or deleted, thus preventing NULL pointer dereferences.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 16/28] nfsd: add data structures for handling CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds data structures and functions to support directory delegation CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server. It introduces two new structs, nfsd_notify_event and nfsd4_cb_notify, which carry fsnotify events and per-delegation state for notification handling respectively. The patch also refactors alloc_init_deleg into a common function with a pluggable callback for freeing resources, and adds alloc_init_dir_deleg to allocate necessary buffers for CB_NOTIFY encoding. Additionally, it sets up skeleton operations for the notification path that will be filled in later.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 17/28] nfsd: add notification handlers for dir events
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds notification handlers for directory events in the NFS daemon (nfsd). It introduces a new fsnotify group for directories and modifies existing code to use this new group when handling directory-related events. The patch also creates a CB_NOTIFY request for directory change events and allocates a nfsd_notify_event object to handle notifications. If any delegation is affected by the event, it recalls the delegation if necessary. This patch aims to improve notification handling in the NFS daemon, particularly for directory changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 18/28] nfsd: add tracepoint to dir_event handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a tracepoint to the dir_event handler in the NFS server (nfsd) code. The purpose of this change is to provide additional visibility into how fsnotify handlers are being called. This can be useful for debugging and understanding the behavior of the nfsd code. Specifically, the new tracepoint will log information about directory events, including the mask of events triggered, the inode number of the affected directory, and the name of the file or subdirectory involved. The patch also includes a new event class declaration in the trace.h header file to support this new tracepoint.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 19/28] nfsd: apply the notify mask to the delegation when requested
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the NFS server to apply a notify mask to directory delegations when requested by the client. If the client requests notifications, the appropriate return mask is set in gddr_notification[0]. The patch also ensures that if the client doesn't request NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, no notifications are offered because the NFS server won't provide necessary information for classic notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 20/28] nfsd: add helper to marshal a fattr4 from completed args
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `nfsd4_encode_attr_vals` to the NFSv4 server code, which encodes attribute values from a completed arguments structure into an XDR stream. The function breaks out the encoding of attribute values into its own separate function, making the code more modular and easier to maintain. This change is part of a larger series of patches that aim to improve the performance and reliability of the NFSv4 server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 21/28] nfsd: allow nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change() to work with no export
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the nfsd4_encode_fattr4_change function to work even when there is no export available, which can occur in certain callback scenarios such as CB_NOTIFY. The change ensures that the function behaves correctly and safely by adding a check for the presence of an export before attempting to access it. This modification addresses a potential issue where the function would previously fail or behave unexpectedly without an export.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 22/28] nfsd: send basic file attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to send basic inode information in a CB_NOTIFY event, including attributes such as type, size, and timestamps. The information is obtained using the GETATTR operation and sent in the form of a JSON object. This allows clients to receive up-to-date file attributes without having to issue additional requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 23/28] nfsd: allow encoding a filehandle into fattr4 without a svc_fh
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the encoding of a filehandle into fattr4 without requiring a svc_fh. It achieves this by adding a new 'fhandle' field to struct nfsd4_fattr_args and copying the filehandle into it from the svc_fh when available. This is necessary for CB_NOTIFY callbacks where the svc_fh is not available.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 24/28] nfsd: add a fi_connectable flag to struct nfs4_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag to the nfs4_file structure called fi_connectable, which indicates whether a filehandle should be encoded as connectable or not. The flag is set based on whether subtree checking is enabled for the export under which a directory delegation was acquired. This change is necessary when encoding a filehandle for a CB_NOTIFY operation where no svc_export is available.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 25/28] nfsd: add the filehandle to returned attributes in CB_NOTIFY
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to return a filehandle in the attributes of a CB_NOTIFY operation in NFSv4. It introduces a new function, setup_notify_fhandle(), which composes a filehandle using only a parent filehandle and an nfs4_file structure. This is necessary because in the context of a CB_NOTIFY, there is no export to consult for composing the filehandle. The patch modifies the nfsd4_setup_notify_entry4() function to call setup_notify_fhandle() and include the filehandle in the attributes if it is successfully composed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 26/28] nfsd: properly track requested child attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of child attributes in NFSv4 delegation by tracking the union of requested and supported attributes, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This ensures that only valid attributes are sent to the client, preventing potential issues with attribute mismatches or inconsistencies. The patch modifies several files in the nfsd directory to implement this change, including nfs4proc.c, nfs4state.c, and nfs4xdr.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 27/28] nfsd: track requested dir attributes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of directory attributes in NFSv4 delegation by tracking the union of requested and supported dir attributes, and only encoding the attributes in that union when sending add/remove/rename updates. This change helps to prevent unnecessary attribute updates and ensures that only valid attributes are sent to clients. The patch modifies several files, including nfsd/nfs4proc.c and fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c, to implement this new behavior.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support to CB_NOTIFY in the NFS server to send notifications when directory attributes change, in addition to add/remove/rename events. The server will still recall the delegation on a SETATTR operation, so these notifications are only sent for changes to child directories. The patch modifies two files: nfsd/nfs4state.c and fs/nfsd/nfs4xdr.c, adding new code to handle directory attribute change notifications and update the notify entry structure accordingly.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] mm: kick writeback flusher instead of inline flush for IOCB_DONTCACHE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance issue in the IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback path by moving writeback submission off the writer's hot path. Instead of submitting writeback inline, it uses a background flusher thread to handle writeback asynchronously and coalescence I/O without explicit skip-if-busy or dirty pressure checks. The patch introduces a new function `filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback` that wakes up the BDI flusher thread with a specific reason for tracing visibility.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ritesh pointed out that the new API `filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback` gives a misleading sense of kicking writeback for only an inode's address space, when in fact it starts writeback for all dirty pages on the respective BDI. He suggested exporting the symbol for `wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()` and using it instead, or modifying the API to make its purpose clearer.
review_comments.raw_body:
This api gives a wrong sense that we are kicking writeback to write
dirty pages which belongs to only this inode's address space mapping.
But instead we are starting wb for everything on the respective bdi.

So instead why not just export symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()
and use it instead?

If not, then IMO at least making it... 
   filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason)

... might be better.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current implementation of filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback causes a WB_SYNC_NONE writeback every time a DONTCACHE write finishes, which is not desired, and suggested introducing a new WB_start_dontcache bit to handle this case.
review_comments.raw_body:
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi ends up calling wb_start_writeback eventually,
which sets WB_start_all, pushes the reason to start_all_reason and then
does the actual wakeup.

The flusher thread then through wb_check_start_all does a WB_SYNC_NONE
writeback based on get_nr_dirty_pages.  Which seems wrong - we don't
want to do a huge writeback evertime the some DONTCACHE write finished.

So I think you'll want a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a new
get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper on a new node counter, etc.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig suggested that the new WB_REASON_DONTCACHE reason for tracing visibility should not be exposed to userspace, and also requested that the function wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() be marked as static in fs-writeback.c.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd rather not expose that to wide with the extra reason arguments
that's not otherwise exposed outside the core writeback code.

Btw, wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi should really be marked static in
fs-writeback.c as well.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary:
Jan Kara noted that the patch could lead to performance regressions if a process creates a large temporary file and deletes it shortly after creation, while another process does small DONTCACHE writes; he suggested this behavior would cause unnecessary allocation, writeback, and deletion of the temporary file

Reviewer Jan Kara expressed concerns about how the patch would handle writeback for inodes with dropbehind set, suggesting either tracking folios per inode or lazily clearing a flag once the inode is clean.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is all true and I can certainly construct a workload where this
behavior will lead to big regressions in performance - for example one
process creating say 1G temporary file and deleting it shortly after
creation + one process doing small DONTCACHE writes. Before this change
only DONTCACHE writes will make it to the disk and temporary file will only
be in the page cache, after this change you pay for allocating, writing
back, and deleting the temporary file.

---

But I'm not sure how you imagine this would work without restricting
writeback to particular inodes. Maybe we could mark inodes which have
folios with dropbehind set and make flush worker only write such inodes?
That would probably require tracking number of folios with dropbehind flag
per inode but that wouldn't be too bad. Or we could just lazily clear this
"inode has dropbehind folios" flag once the inode is clean.

								Honza

-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggested that instead of waking up the flusher thread to drain all dirty pages, it should only expedite writing by the number of pending dontcache folios, which would still write the least recently dirtied inodes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd only expedite writing by the number of pending dontcache folios.
It would still write the least recently dirtied inodes.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the new writeback mechanism may cause dontcache folios to stay cached for a long time if mixed with normal writes, but agreed that it might level out over longer periods.
review_comments.raw_body:
So that may result in dontcache folios staying cached for quite some time
if DONTCACHE and normal writes are mixed. But I agree over longer time it
might just level out so maybe that's fine.

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern that IOCB_DONTCACHE on writes doesn't provide guarantees about when the writeback will be done, and clarified that it only means 'don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback'.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think that's probably OK. In my interpretation, DONTCACHE on write
just means "don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback".
It doesn't provide any guarantees about when the writeback will be
done.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the performance of kicking the flusher thread for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, specifically that forcing out a single inode's pages instead of allowing the thread to batch them leads to worse performance. The author has drafted an alternative version of the patch but decided not to pursue it due to its inferior results.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ritesh suggested an alternative approach to reducing dirty page cache pressure by tracking the number of dontcache pages and kicking the flusher for those many target pages, which would still help with batching but may not always write only dontcache marked folios.
review_comments.raw_body:
So, if I understood it correctly, Christoph might be talking about a
different approach here.
Instead of kicking flusher thread to writeback pages for a single inode,
if we can track the number of dontcache pages
(get_nr_dontcache_pages()), then we can kick the flusher for those many
target pages. I think this way we are still reducing the dirty page
cache pressure - the problem which RWF_DONTCACHE is supposed to solve.
But I guess, that doesn't necessarily always mean that only dontcache
marked folios will get written.

If we implement that then, this should still help with the batching
problem you mentioned and hopefully should not cause a major regression
for the workload which Jan mentioned.

Feel free to correct my understanding here please.

-ritesh

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite, nfsd-io-bench, to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and libnfs against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes various workloads such as sequential and random read/write, multi-writer, noisy-neighbor, and latency-sensitive reader tests. It also includes scripts for setting up the NFS export, running the benchmarks, and parsing the results from fio's JSON output. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive testing framework for NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. This patch aims to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for specific I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the performance of the per-inode writeback approach, specifically that it regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads due to lost I/O batching. The author acknowledges this regression and suggests sticking with v2, which has a different approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, that version regressed performance in a couple of cases. I think v2 is probably the best approach, on balance. Maybe we can get this into -next so that it can make v7.2?

Here's the comparison between this version and a writeback_single_inode() flush version:

------------------8<-----------------------

● Comparing dontcache numbers against the previous whole-BDI flusher kernel (from /tmp/dontcache-local-4way-flusher.md):                                                                          

  Per-Inode vs Whole-BDI Flusher — DONTCACHE on Local XFS                                                                                                                                         

  Single-Client Writes

  ┌──────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐                                                                                                                                      
  │    Benchmark     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │                                                                                                                                      
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤      
  │ Seq write MB/s   │ 1450      │ 1438      │ -1% (noise) │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Seq write p99.9  │ 23.5 ms   │ 23.5 ms   │ identical   │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write MB/s  │ 363       │ 286       │ -21%        │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write p99.9 │ 1.8 ms    │ 16.7 ms   │ regression  │
  └──────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Seq write is identical. Rand write regressed — the whole-BDI flusher batched all dirty pages in one pass with writeback_sb_inodes() under a single blk_plug, while per-inode write_inode_now() loses that batching.

  Single-Client Reads

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────┐
  │   Benchmark    │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │ Change │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Seq read MB/s  │ 2950      │ 2350      │ -20%   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Rand read MB/s │ 651       │ 519       │ -20%   │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────┘

  Reads shouldn't be affected by writeback path changes. Buffered reads also dropped (2888 → 2331), suggesting different system conditions between runs rather than a per-inode regression.

  Multi-Writer (Scenario A)

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────────┐
  │     Metric     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ Aggregate MB/s │ 1478      │ 999       │ -32%       │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ p99.9          │ 46 ms     │ 77 ms     │ -67% worse │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────┘

  This is the biggest regression. With whole-BDI, the flusher did one batched pass through all dirty inodes via writeback_sb_inodes(). With per-inode, each of 4 writers queues a separate work item processed serially by write_inode_now() — losing the batch I/O merging benefit.

  Scenario C & D (Noisy Neighbor)

  ┌─────────────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐
  │         Metric          │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C writer       │ 1468      │ 1386      │ -6%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C readers      │ 18.7 MB/s │ 18.7 MB/s │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D writer       │ 1472      │ 1467      │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D readers      │ 496 MB/s  │ 507 MB/s  │ +2%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D reader p99.9 │ 440 us    │ 358 us    │ +19% better │
  └─────────────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Mixed-mode (Scenario D) is the intended production case and it's essentially identical or slightly better — per-inode writeback creates less device contention for buffered readers.

  Summary

  The per-inode approach is neutral-to-slightly-better for the production scenario (Scenario D), but regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads. The core issue is loss of I/O batching
 — writeback_sb_inodes() processes all dirty inodes in one blk_plug'd pass, while per-inode write_inode_now() calls are processed one at a time. The read regressions likely reflect different
  system conditions since buffered/direct reads also dropped ~20%.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary: Ritesh pointed out that the current API gives a misleading impression of kicking writeback for only an inode's address space mapping, when in fact it starts writeback for all dirty pages on the respective bdi. He suggested exporting the symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() and using it instead, or alternatively modifying filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason) to better reflect its behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
This api gives a wrong sense that we are kicking writeback to write
dirty pages which belongs to only this inode's address space mapping.
But instead we are starting wb for everything on the respective bdi.

So instead why not just export symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()
and use it instead?

If not, then IMO at least making it... 
   filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason)

... might be better.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the current implementation of wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi causes WB_SYNC_NONE writeback to occur every time a DONTCACHE write finishes, which is inefficient and unnecessary. He suggested introducing a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper function, and other related changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi ends up calling wb_start_writeback eventually,
which sets WB_start_all, pushes the reason to start_all_reason and then
does the actual wakeup.

The flusher thread then through wb_check_start_all does a WB_SYNC_NONE
writeback based on get_nr_dirty_pages.  Which seems wrong - we don't
want to do a huge writeback evertime the some DONTCACHE write finished.

So I think you'll want a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a new
get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper on a new node counter, etc.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig requested that the extra reason arguments for wide wakeups not be exposed outside the core writeback code, and also suggested marking wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi static in fs-writeback.c
review_comments.raw_body:
I'd rather not expose that to wide with the extra reason arguments
that's not otherwise exposed outside the core writeback code.

Btw, wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi should really be marked static in
fs-writeback.c as well.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the benchmark results provided in the patch description are incomplete and potentially stale, suggesting they be included directly in the patch for better reproducibility. Additionally, he questioned the validity of certain benchmark numbers, specifically the read changes between patches and unpatched dontcache in XFS and NFSD, requesting further investigation into their causes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Please add the results into the patch.  Besides having them in the patch
vs the cover letter, URLs to random hosting sites tend to get stale
very easily.

Comments on the XFS numbers / benchmark setup:

How does O_DIRECT manage to create almost the same peek dirty as
buffered?  Similarly even this patched doncache show be lower, which
feels odd.

(the editorializing in these results feels odd, not sure what tool
generates them, but maybe edit it for the commit log to look a bit more
serious).

Given that this patch does not change the read path, how do the pure read
numbers for patches vs unpatched dontcache manage to differ so much?

Comments on the NFSD numbers / benchmark setup:

I though you were testing using dontcache on the server, but the
comments seem to imply it is done on the client, what is the case
here? 

Again, the read changes between patches and unpatched look suspect.
Please drill down why they happen, the fact that patches is slower here
and faster in XFS makes me wonder if the results might just be very
volatile?

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that limiting writeback to a specific inode has several drawbacks, which explains the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE in write-heavy workloads.

review_comments.raw_body:
Limiting writeback to a specific inode has many downsides as I explained
